<content>
1: ---
2: title: "System Spec Kit"
3: description: "Unified documentation and context preservation skill: spec folder workflows (Levels 1-3+), Level contract templates, validation scripts and Spec Kit Memory MCP with hybrid multi-channel retrieval."
4: trigger_phrases:
5:   - "spec kit"
6:   - "spec folder"
7:   - "memory system"
8:   - "hybrid search"
9:   - "context preservation"
10:   - "documentation levels"
11:   - "memory save"
12:   - "spec folder workflow"
13: ---
14: 
15: # System Spec Kit
16: 
17: > Documentation and memory for AI-assisted development. Every file change gets a spec folder. Every session gets persistent context.
18: 
19: ---
20: 
21: ## 1. OVERVIEW
22: 
23: ### What System Spec Kit Does
24: 
25: System Spec Kit solves two problems that every AI-assisted project runs into.
26: 
27: First, AI conversations that modify files leave no paper trail. A feature gets built, the session ends and the reasoning behind every decision vanishes. Spec Kit fixes this by creating a **spec folder** for every file-modifying conversation. The folder is a numbered directory with a specification, plan, task list and implementation summary that documents what changed and why.
28: 
29: Second, AI assistants have amnesia. Every conversation starts from a blank slate. You explain your architecture on Monday and by Wednesday the assistant has forgotten everything. Spec Kit fixes this with a **persistent memory system**. An MCP server stores decisions, context and project history in a local SQLite database so the next session can pick up where the last one left off, regardless of which AI model or tool you use.
30: 
31: Together, these two halves form a documentation-and-memory loop. Spec folders capture what happened, the indexed-continuity store makes it searchable, and the next session benefits from everything that came before.
32: 
33: > Note: When this skill says "memory," it means our local indexed-continuity store. The SQLite-backed spec-doc record index ships with this skill. It is **not** Anthropic Claude Memory (the managed product surfaced in claude.ai). It is **not** the MCP reference `memory` server (the upstream community example). Identifiers (`memory_*` MCP tools, `/memory:*` slash commands, `memory_*` SQL tables, `memory-*.ts` handlers, `references/memory/`, `MEMORY_*` constants, `_memory:` frontmatter) are frozen by REQ-001. The disambiguation lives in operator-facing prose only.
34: 
35: ### How This Compares
36: 
37: Manual documentation is ad hoc and inconsistent. Basic RAG offers vector similarity over a stateless index. System Spec Kit replaces both with templated spec folders at 4 levels, validated structure, and a 5-channel hybrid search fused via Reciprocal Rank Fusion.
38: 
39: Context survives across sessions through persistent semantic memory and session recovery rather than copy-paste from notes. Quality control runs through a 3-layer save gate, 20-rule validation and DQI scoring instead of relying on human review alone. "Why" queries are answered by a causal graph with 6 relationship types instead of grepping commit messages. Forgetting follows an FSRS power-law decay tuned by content type and importance, not a flat "remember everything" or naive exponential. Access control uses shared memory with deny-by-default membership instead of filesystem permissions alone.
40: 
41: ### Key Features at a Glance
42: 
43: - **Spec Folder Workflow**. Creates mandatory documentation for every file-modifying conversation, scaled to 4 levels based on scope and risk, with packet-local changelog closeout for packet roots and child phases.
44: - **Level Contract Templates**. Manifest template architecture where each level resolves the files and sections it needs.
45: - **Spec Kit Memory MCP**. 37-tool MCP server providing persistent semantic memory, causal and degree retrieval, and session orchestration across sessions, models and tools.
46: - **Startup and Recovery Surfaces**. `/speckit:resume` is the canonical operator-facing recovery surface. Under the hood, startup and recovery rebuild active context from `handover.md`, then `_memory.continuity`, then canonical spec docs.
47: - **Session Continuity**. `generate-context.js` updates canonical continuity surfaces and refreshes packet metadata on every `/memory:save` invocation so `/speckit:resume` can rebuild the next session from packet-local sources.
48: - **Validation Scripts**. 20-rule validation, continuity freshness checks, and strict EVIDENCE-marker linting for spec folders.
49: - **Phase Decomposition**. Parent and child spec folder structure for multi-session, multi-phase work.
50: - **Constitutional Memory**. Always-surface rules with a 3.0x boost that never decay, like pinned notes that show up in every search.
51: - **Shared Memory**. Controlled knowledge sharing with deny-by-default access for teams and multi-agent setups.
52: 
53: ### Requirements
54: 
55: Requires Node.js >= 20.11, TypeScript 5.0+, and Bash 4.0+. Embeddings are **local-first** (ADR-014, 2026-05-19): the runtime probes Ollama first (default, `nomic-embed-text`, 768-dim), falls through to pure-Node hf-local (`@huggingface/transformers` HTTP model server), and only escalates to OpenAI or Voyage when an API key is set AND no local tier is available.
56: 
57: **Recommended new-user setup:** install [Ollama](https://ollama.com) and run `ollama pull nomic-embed-text:v1.5`. The cascade auto-detects it on first daemon startup and persists `nomic-embed-text-v1.5` (768d) as the active embedder — no API keys required, all embeddings stay on-device.
58: 
59: Workspace module profile:
60: 
61: - `shared/` and `mcp_server/` are ESM packages (`"type": "module"`) using NodeNext TypeScript settings.
62: - `scripts/` is also ESM (`"type": "module"`), with built CLI entrypoints under `dist/` for shell workflows and cross-package loading.
63: 
64: ---
65: 
66: ## 2. QUICK START
67: 
68: ### Create Your First Spec Folder
69: 
70: When an AI assistant asks "Which spec folder?" at Gate 3, choose Option B (New) to create one:
71: 
72: ```bash
73: # Create a Level 1 spec folder
74: bash .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/scripts/spec/create.sh 042-my-feature
75: 
76: # Creates: specs/042-my-feature/
77: # Files: spec.md, plan.md, tasks.md (Level 1 starters)
78: ```
79: 
80: The script sets up the folder, copies the right templates for the chosen level, initializes `description.json`, and prepares the packet docs plus `scratch/` workspace. Continuity no longer writes to `[spec]/memory/*.md`; use `/memory:save` to route updates into canonical packet docs such as `implementation-summary.md`, `decision-record.md`, and `handover.md`.
81: 
82: ### Save Context at the End of a Session
83: 
84: When your work session ends, save what happened so the next session can continue:
85: 
86: ```bash
87: # Update the canonical continuity surfaces from structured JSON
88: node .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/scripts/dist/memory/generate-context.js \
89:   --json '{"specFolder":"042-my-feature","user_prompts":["Implement login form validation"],"observations":["Added client-side validation for empty email and password"],"recent_context":["Touched auth form schema and submit handler"],"toolCalls":["npm test -- auth"],"exchanges":["Verified the error states render before submit"]}' \
90:   specs/042-my-feature/
91: 
92: # Result: canonical continuity surfaces updated for the target spec folder
93: ```
94: 
95: Or use the command shorthand:
96: 
97: ```text
98: /memory:save 042-my-feature
99: ```
100: 
101: Every canonical save now refreshes `description.json.lastUpdated` and `graph-metadata.json.derived.*`, so the default `/memory:save` path is no longer a metadata no-op.
102: 
103: ### Resume Work From a Previous Session
104: 
105: Start a new session on work you did before:
106: 
107: ```text
108: /speckit:resume
109: ```
110: 
111: The system rebuilds continuation context in a fixed order: `handover.md` first, then `_memory.continuity`, then the packet's canonical spec docs. It presents the current state, prior decisions, touched files, and next steps before you start.
112: 
113: ### Search for Context
114: 
115: Ask the indexed-continuity store a question in plain language:
116: 
117: ```text
118: /memory:search "how did we decide on the auth architecture?"
119: ```
120: 
121: The system reads your question, figures out you are looking for a past decision and routes to the right search strategy automatically.
122: 
123: ### Validate a Spec Folder
124: 
125: ```bash
126: # Run all 20 validation rules
127: bash .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/scripts/spec/validate.sh \
128:   .opencode/specs/[project]/042-my-feature/
129: 
130: # Exit 0 = success, Exit 1 = user error, Exit 2 = validation error, Exit 3 = system error
131: ```
132: 
133: ### Verify the MCP Is Running
134: 
135: Check that Spec Kit Memory tools are available:
136: 
137: ```json
138: {
139:   "tool": "memory_health",
140:   "arguments": { "reportMode": "full" }
141: }
142: ```
143: 
144: The response should return `status: "ok"` and database table counts. If it returns an error, see [Troubleshooting](#7-troubleshooting).
145: 
146: Codex CLI note: if the MCP server runs in a restricted or read-only repo context, point `SPEC_KIT_DB_DIR` at a writable directory such as one under your home folder or `/tmp`. Use `MEMORY_DB_PATH` only when you intentionally need one fixed sqlite file.
147: 
148: ---
149: 
150: ## 3. FEATURES
151: 
152: ### 3.1 SPEC FOLDER WORKFLOWS
153: 
154: #### What Spec Folders Are
155: 
156: A spec folder is a numbered directory (like `specs/042-my-feature/`) that holds the documentation for a single unit of work. Think of it as a project folder for an AI conversation -- it keeps the specification, plan, task list and implementation summary together so the reasoning behind every change is preserved. For packet roots and direct child phases, closeout can also produce packet-local changelog entries that live beside the packet instead of only in the global release stream.
157: 
158: Every conversation that modifies files gets a spec folder. This is enforced by Gate 3 in the project's AGENTS.md -- the AI assistant asks "Which spec folder?" before any file modification begins. The only exemptions are single-file fixes under 5 characters (typo or whitespace corrections).
159: 
160: #### Documentation Levels
161: 
162: Not every change needs the same amount of paperwork. A one-line bug fix does not need an architecture decision record. A multi-system refactor does. Spec Kit uses four levels to match documentation depth to task complexity.
163: 
164: | Level  | LOC Guidance   | Required Files                                        | When to Use                                          |
165: | ------ | -------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |
166: | **1**  | < 100          | spec.md, plan.md, tasks.md, implementation-summary.md | Small features, bug fixes, single-file changes       |
167: | **2**  | 100 - 499      | Level 1 + checklist.md                                | Features needing QA verification, multi-file changes |
168: | **3**  | 500+           | Level 2 + decision-record.md                          | Architecture changes, complex refactors              |
169: | **3+** | Complexity 80+ | Level 3 + approval workflow, compliance, stakeholders  | High-complexity work needing review tracking          |
170: | **Phase Parent** | n/a (control file only) | spec.md, description.json, graph-metadata.json | Folder contains phase children (`[0-9]{3}-name/` subdirs with their own spec.md/description.json) |
171: 
172: The LOC ranges are guidance, not hard rules. Risk, complexity and the number of affected files can push a task to a higher level. When in doubt, choose the higher level.
173: 
174: **Implementation-summary.md** is required at all levels but created **after** implementation completes, not at spec folder creation time.
175: 
176: Packet-local changelogs are additive, not a replacement for `implementation-summary.md`. When the target is a packet root or direct child phase, `/speckit:implement`, `/speckit:complete`, and the nested changelog workflow can write packet history into a local `changelog/` directory using the canonical root/phase naming rules.
177: 
178: #### Spec Folder Structure
179: 
180: When `create.sh` builds a spec folder, it produces this layout:
181: 
182: ```text
183: specs/<###-feature-name>/
184: ├── description.json             # Spec identity and memory tracking metadata
185: ├── spec.md                      # What the feature is and why it exists
186: ├── plan.md                      # How to implement it
187: ├── tasks.md                     # Step-by-step task breakdown
188: ├── checklist.md                 # QA validation gates (Level 2+)
189: ├── decision-record.md           # Architecture decisions (Level 3+)
190: ├── implementation-summary.md    # Post-implementation summary (all levels)
191: ├── handover.md                  # Operator-facing session handoff for /speckit:resume
192: ├── resource-map.md              # Optional lean path catalog (any level)
193: ├── changelog/                   # Packet-local changelog history for packet roots / phase parents
194: └── scratch/                     # Temporary workspace files (gitignored)
195: ```
196: 
197: `generate-context.js` updates the packet's continuity state for `/speckit:resume`, refreshes `description.json.lastUpdated`, and rewrites `graph-metadata.json` derived fields on every canonical save; recovery then rebuilds context from `handover.md`, `_memory.continuity`, and the packet docs.
198: 
199: **Phase parents** are an exception. When a folder contains phase children (matching `^[0-9]{3}-[a-z0-9-]+$` with their own `spec.md` or `description.json`), the parent only requires the **lean trio**: `spec.md`, `description.json`, `graph-metadata.json`. Heavy docs (`plan.md`, `tasks.md`, `checklist.md`, `decision-record.md`, `implementation-summary.md`) live exclusively in the children where they stay accurate to that phase's actual work. The parent's `spec.md` carries a Phase Documentation Map; the parent's `graph-metadata.json` carries `derived.last_active_child_id` + `derived.last_active_at` pointer fields that the generator atomically updates on every save (parent saves write `null`; child saves bubble up the child's `packet_id`). `/speckit:resume` reads the pointer first when the target is a phase parent. A fresh pointer (<24h) recurses directly into the active child. A stale or missing pointer falls back to listing children with statuses. Detection is a single source of truth: `is_phase_parent()` (shell) and `isPhaseParent()` (ESM JS) MUST agree.
200: 
201: #### Checklist Priority System (Level 2+)
202: 
203: Checklists use a priority system so reviewers know what blocks shipping and what can wait:
204: 
205: | Priority | Meaning                                                 | Deferral                        |
206: | -------- | ------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------- |
207: | **P0**   | Hard blocker -- cannot ship without this                | Cannot defer                    |
208: | **P1**   | Required -- must complete or get user approval to defer | Needs explicit approval to skip |
209: | **P2**   | Optional -- nice to have                                | Can defer without approval      |
210: 
211: #### Phase Decomposition
212: 
213: When a feature is too large for a single spec folder, use phase decomposition to split it into parent and child folders. The parent holds the overall specification. Each child holds one phase of the work.
214: 
215: ```text
216: specs/022-big-feature/             # Parent spec folder
217: ├── spec.md                        # Overall specification
218: ├── 001-data-model/                # Phase 1 child
219: │   ├── spec.md
220: │   └── ...
221: ├── 002-api-endpoints/             # Phase 2 child
222: │   ├── spec.md
223: │   └── ...
224: └── 003-frontend/                  # Phase 3 child
225:     ├── spec.md
226:     └── ...
227: ```
228: 
229: Use `create.sh --phase` to create a parent with its first child in one step. The parent and child folders render from the manifest template source through the Level contract resolver. Run `validate.sh --recursive` to validate the parent and all children together; the validator's phase-parent branch automatically skips Level-N expectations on the lean parent (rules with branches: `check-files.sh`, `check-level-match.sh`, `check-anchors.sh`, `check-section-counts.sh`, `check-template-headers.sh`).
230: 
231: Tolerant migration policy: legacy phase parents that retain heavy docs continue to validate without churn. Soft deprecation is a separate follow-on packet.
232: 
233: #### Validation
234: 
235: The `validate.sh` script runs 20+ rules against a spec folder and reports what passes and what needs fixing. Rules check for required files, template compliance, placeholder detection, anchor markers and cross-reference consistency. The new `PHASE_PARENT_CONTENT` rule (severity: warn) scans phase-parent `spec.md` for forbidden migration-history tokens (consolidation/merge/rename narratives) and is code-fence + HTML-comment aware. In strict flows, the validation surface includes `_memory.continuity` freshness checks plus strict `EVIDENCE` marker linting, with the bracket-depth audit script available for repair sweeps before rerunning validation.
236: 
237: | Exit Code | Meaning          | Action                              |
238: | --------- | ---------------- | ----------------------------------- |
239: | 0         | All rules pass   | Ready to proceed                    |
240: | 1         | User error       | Fix the flag or input and retry     |
241: | 2         | Validation error | Fix the spec folder before claiming completion |
242: | 3         | System error     | Check file I/O, missing manifest, or environment |
243: 
244: Run with `--verbose` to see the details behind each rule, or `--recursive` to validate a parent and all child phase folders.
245: 
246: ---
247: 
248: <!-- divider:4.2 -->
249: 
250: ### 3.2 MEMORY SYSTEM
251: 
252: The indexed-continuity store lives in an MCP server that gives AI assistants persistent memory across sessions, models and tools. It stores context in a local SQLite database and retrieves exactly what is relevant when a new session starts.
253: 
254: Think of it like a personal librarian that keeps notes on every conversation, files them by topic and hands you the right ones when you start a new task. Switch from Claude to GPT to Gemini and back -- the spec-doc record stays the same because it lives on your machine, not inside any AI's context window.
255: 
256: For full architecture details, the 37-tool API reference, search pipeline internals and configuration, see [`mcp_server/README.md`](./mcp_server/README.md).
257: 
258: #### Hybrid Search
259: 
260: When you search, the system checks five sources at once -- like a librarian who checks the card catalog, the shelf labels, the reading room sign-out sheet, the recommendation board and the "related topics" corkboard all at the same time.
261: 
262: | Channel          | How It Works                                        | Good For                                       |
263: | ---------------- | --------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
264: | **Vector**       | Compares meaning via embeddings (`nomic-embed-text-v1.5` 768d local default) | Finding related content even when words differ |
265: | **FTS5**         | Full-text search on exact words and phrases         | Specific terms and error messages              |
266: | **BM25**         | Keyword relevance scoring                           | Ranking when you know roughly what you want    |
267: | **Causal Graph** | Follows cause-and-effect links between memories     | "Why did we choose this?" questions            |
268: | **Degree**       | Scores by graph connectivity, weighted by edge type | Finding important hub decisions                |
269: 
270: Results from all channels are combined using Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) with a K parameter tuned per query intent. A spec-doc record that scores well in multiple channels rises to the top.
271: 
272: #### Search Pipeline
273: 
274: Every search goes through four stages, like an assembly line where each station has one clear job:
275: 
276: 1. **Gather** -- collect candidates from active channels in parallel. Constitutional memories are always injected regardless of score.
277: 2. **Score** -- fuse channel results with RRF, then apply 8 post-fusion scoring signals (co-activation boost, FSRS decay, interference penalty, cold-start boost, session recency, causal 2-hop, intent weights, channel min-representation).
278: 3. **Rerank** -- apply MMR diversity reranking (algorithmic, no model) to reduce near-duplicate results, then collapse chunks back to parent memories.
279: 4. **Filter** -- enforce score immutability, apply state filtering, annotate with confidence labels (high/medium/low) and truncate at the confidence gap.
280: 
281: #### Query Intelligence
282: 
283: Before any search runs, the system figures out what kind of help you need -- like a triage nurse who reads your symptoms and routes you to the right specialist.
284: 
285: - **Complexity routing** sizes up your question and picks how many channels to use (2 for simple, 4 for moderate, all 5 for complex)
286: - **Intent classification** maps your query to one of 7 task types (add_feature, fix_bug, refactor, security_audit, understand, find_spec, find_decision), each with its own channel weight profile
287: - **Query decomposition** splits multi-topic questions into focused sub-queries without needing an LLM call
288: - **HyDE fallback** writes a hypothetical answer to your question, then searches for real documents matching it -- surfaces content your original wording missed
289: 
290: #### Memory Lifecycle
291: 
292: Not all spec-doc records are equally useful forever. The system uses FSRS (Free Spaced Repetition Scheduler) to track freshness -- a decay model validated on 100M+ Anki flashcard users.
293: 
294: | Tier               | Description                       | Decay Behavior                           |
295: | ------------------ | --------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
296: | **Constitutional** | Always-surface rules (3.0x boost) | Never decays                             |
297: | **Critical**       | High-importance decisions         | Never decays or decays at 2x slower rate |
298: | **Important**      | Significant patterns              | 1.5x slower than normal                  |
299: | **Normal**         | Standard session context          | Standard FSRS decay                      |
300: | **Temporary**      | Quick scratch notes               | Fast decay                               |
301: | **Deprecated**     | Superseded content                | Fastest decay                            |
302: 
303: Decay speed is also controlled by content type (decisions decay slower than general notes). Memories earn promotions through positive feedback: 5 thumbs-up promotes normal to important, 10 promotes to critical.
304: 
305: Four active cognitive states track access patterns: **HOT** (just used), **WARM**, **COLD**, and **DORMANT**. Hot memories get full content in results. Warm ones appear as summaries. Cold and dormant content only surfaces if it still scores well enough.
306: 
307: #### Causal Graph
308: 
309: The system tracks how decisions relate to each other -- like a corkboard with sticky notes connected by string. One note says "we chose JWT tokens." A string connects it to "because the session store was too slow." Another string connects that to "the Redis outage on March 5th."
310: 
311: Six relationship types: `caused`, `enabled`, `supersedes`, `contradicts`, `derived_from`, `supports`. Community detection (Louvain algorithm) automatically clusters related memories so finding one surfaces its neighbors.
312: 
313: #### Causal Trust Display Badges (012/005)
314: 
315: `memory_search` results now carry an additive `trustBadges` payload per `MemoryResultEnvelope`. The badges read existing causal-edge columns at response time, so callers can judge whether a causal claim looks fresh and well-supported without changing storage.
316: 
317: | Badge | Source |
318: |-------|--------|
319: | `confidence` | clamped from the strongest connected edge `strength` |
320: | `extractionAge` | human-readable age from the newest connected `extracted_at` |
321: | `lastAccessAge` | human-readable age from the newest connected `last_accessed` |
322: | `orphan` | `true` when the result has no incoming causal edges |
323: | `weightHistoryChanged` | `true` when any connected edge has a `weight_history` row |
324: 
325: The formatter at `mcp_server/formatters/search-results.ts` batch-derives the badges from existing causal-edge tables, fails open when the DB handle or `weight_history` table is unavailable, and preserves any precomputed `trustBadges` payload a caller already supplied. Response-profile shaping in `mcp_server/lib/response/profile-formatters.ts` preserves the badge payload through `quick`, `research`, and `resume` outputs on `results[]` and `topResult` rather than dropping it. Display only: no schema change, no new relation types, no new storage of code/process/tool facts (ADR-012-005).
326: 
327: #### Save Intelligence
328: 
329: When you save new content, the system runs an arbitration process before storing anything. Prediction Error gating compares incoming content against existing spec-doc records and picks one of four outcomes:
330: 
331: | Outcome       | When                               | What Happens                                |
332: | ------------- | ---------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
333: | **CREATE**    | Nothing similar exists             | Stored as new knowledge                     |
334: | **REINFORCE** | Similar exists, new one adds value | Both kept, existing gets a confidence boost |
335: | **UPDATE**    | Similar exists, new one is better  | Old version replaced in place               |
336: | **SUPERSEDE** | New knowledge contradicts the old  | New version active, old one demoted         |
337: 
338: Three quality gates run before storage: structure check (required format and metadata), semantic sufficiency check (enough real content to be useful), and duplicate detection.
339: Short decision-type memories can bypass the content-length gate when SPECKIT_SAVE_QUALITY_GATE_EXCEPTIONS=true and at least two structural signals (title, specFolder, or anchor) are present.
340: 
341: #### Embedding Reconciliation
342: 
343: `memory_embedding_reconcile` is a maintenance tool on the mk-spec-memory surface. It converges `embedding_status` for vector-present stale rows and resets genuinely missing-vector retry rows inside one guarded `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` transaction. The default mode is `dry-run`: no writes happen unless `mode: "apply"` is passed. Use it after an embedder swap or after the health report shows a high pending/failed count.
344: 
345: ```json
346: { "tool": "memory_embedding_reconcile", "arguments": { "mode": "dry-run" } }
347: ```
348: 
349: #### Index Scan Self-Maintaining Behavior
350: 
351: `memory_index_scan` is self-maintaining from spec 026 onward. Overlapping scan calls return a `coalesced: true` success envelope instead of a raw E429 error. Rows become BM25/FTS-searchable immediately as `pending` while vectors drain (`complete_with_pending_vectors` with a `pendingVectors` count), so content is always text-searchable even when the embedding queue is backed up. Move reconciliation heals renamed spec folders by packet identity without re-embedding. Each scan also runs a bounded global orphan sweep.
352: 
353: `memory_health` now includes an `index` block with a `summary` enum and row counts:
354: 
355: | Summary value | Meaning |
356: | --- | --- |
357: | `healthy_fresh` | Index is current and all vectors are resolved |
358: | `healthy_lagging_vectors` | Index is current but some vectors are still pending |
359: | `stale_needs_scan` | Index has not been scanned recently |
360: | `degraded_needs_repair` | Failed rows require `memory_embedding_reconcile` |
361: | `unavailable` | Index state could not be read |
362: 
363: #### Index Schema History (v28 -> v30) and the `.needs-rebuild` Sentinel
364: 
365: The SQLite index schema (`mcp_server/lib/search/vector-index-schema.ts`, `SCHEMA_VERSION = 30`) advanced three migrations during the 013 roadmap. Each is additive and applied automatically at server boot:
366: 
367: | Migration | Adds | Effect |
368: | --- | --- | --- |
369: | **v28** | Active-row partial unique index `idx_memory_logical_key_active_unique` | Enforces one non-deprecated row per logical key (spec folder + canonical path + anchor + tenant/user/agent/session), excluding `constitutional` and `deprecated` tiers. Backs the deprecate-before-insert dedup guard so a re-index can no longer create a duplicate active row. |
370: | **v29** | Checkpoint-v2 metadata columns `snapshot_format`, `snapshot_path` | Lets a checkpoint record a file-snapshot format and on-disk path so checkpoint-v2 can restore from a `VACUUM ... INTO` snapshot rather than an inline blob. |
371: | **v30** | Enrichment marker columns `post_insert_enrichment_status`, `post_insert_enrichment_state`, `post_insert_enrichment_completed_at`, `post_insert_enrichment_version` + partial index `idx_post_insert_enrichment_incomplete` | Tracks whether the optional save-time enrichment bundle finished for a row, so incomplete enrichment can be found and resumed without re-saving. |
372: 
373: After a checkpoint restore that swaps the live DB files, the runtime writes a `.needs-rebuild` sentinel (`NEEDS_REBUILD_SENTINEL_NAME` in `mcp_server/lib/storage/checkpoints.ts`) beside the restored DB. The next boot detects it through `repairNeedsRebuildSentinel()` and rebuilds the derived indexes (FTS5/BM25 shadow and vector profile) before serving, so a restored snapshot never serves from a stale shadow. The sentinel is cleared once the rebuild completes.
374: 
375: #### MCP Front-Proxy and In-Place Daemon Recycle
376: 
377: The mk-spec-memory launcher fronts the backend daemon with a session proxy (`bridgeStdioThroughSessionProxy` in `.opencode/bin/mk-spec-memory-launcher.cjs`, implemented in `.opencode/bin/lib/launcher-session-proxy.cjs`). The proxy keeps one stable client-facing stdio session while the backend behind it can be recycled in place -- for example when the RSS-ceiling watchdog (`SPECKIT_LAUNCHER_RSS_SELF_EXIT`) restarts the daemon, or when a new build replaces the backend. Read-only replayable tools (`memory_search`, `memory_context`, and similar) are transparently retried across a recycle so a routine restart looks like a brief pause rather than a hard failure to the caller.
378: 
379: This recycle/reconnect behavior surfaces three operator-visible error codes:
380: 
381: | Code | Retryable | Meaning |
382: | --- | --- | --- |
383: | `E429` | Legacy | The former index rate-limit class. The self-maintaining `memory_index_scan` contract replaced it with a `coalesced: true` success envelope, so a routine overlapping scan no longer returns `E429`. |
384: | `-32001` | **Yes** | `RETRYABLE_RECYCLE_ERROR` -- the **live** launcher recycle signal (`backend recycled; retry`, `data.retryable: true`). The front-proxy emits it when the backend is recycling under it; clients retry and reconnect. This code is **not removed**: only the index vector-drain *outage* path stopped surfacing its own `-32001` class. |
385: | `-32002` | No | `PROTOCOL_MISMATCH_ERROR` -- a fail-closed protocol break (`backend protocol version changed; client reconnect required`, `data.retryable: false`). The proxy moves to a terminal CLOSED state and the client must reconnect from scratch; it is never auto-retried. |
386: 
387: #### Evaluation Infrastructure
388: 
389: The indexed-continuity store includes built-in tools for measuring search quality:
390: 
391: - **Ablation studies** -- turn off one search component at a time to measure its contribution, like removing one ingredient from a recipe to see if the dish still tastes good
392: - **12-metric computation** -- MRR, NDCG, MAP and 9 other information retrieval metrics
393: - **Synthetic ground truth corpus** -- 110 test questions with known correct answers for benchmarking, keyed to live parent-memory IDs; rerun `scripts/evals/map-ground-truth-ids.ts` after DB rebuilds or imports before trusting ablation or reporting comparisons
394: - **Reporting dashboard** -- performance trends across work periods and search channels, sourced from parent-memory-normalized eval rows even when retrieval hits came from chunks
395:

400: ### 3.3 COMMANDS
401: 
402: Spec Kit exposes its core workflow through 4 `/speckit:*` commands (`complete`, `implement`, `plan`, `resume`; `plan` also has an `--intake-only` flag variant) plus 4 `/memory:*` operations. Repository-wide command entry points also include 6 `/deep:*` loop commands (one of which, `start-agent-improvement-loop`, supersedes the former `improve` commands), 7 `/create:*` commands, 3 `/doctor:*` commands, and the `agent_router` and `prompt` utilities. Each command opens access to a specific set of tools.
403: 
404: #### Spec Kit Commands (4)
405: 
406: | Command                 | Steps | Purpose                                                                                                                          |
407: | ----------------------- | ----- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
408: | `/speckit:plan --intake-only` | N/A   | Standalone intake interview that publishes `spec.md`, `description.json`, and `graph-metadata.json`                        |
409: | `/speckit:complete`    | 14    | Full end-to-end workflow: spec through implementation, verification, and packet-local changelog closeout, with the shared intake contract from [intake_contract.md](./references/workflows/intake_contract.md) when intake is still needed |
410: | `/speckit:plan`        | 7     | Planning only -- spec through plan, no implementation, with the shared intake contract from [intake_contract.md](./references/workflows/intake_contract.md) for `no-spec`, `partial-folder`, `repair-mode`, or `placeholder-upgrade` packets |
411: | `/speckit:implement`   | 9     | Execute pre-planned work. Requires existing `plan.md`; packet-aware targets also generate local changelog output during closeout |
412: | `/speckit:resume`      | 4     | Resume a previous session on an existing spec folder                                                                             |
413: | `/deep:start-research-loop` | N/A | Autonomous research loop with convergence detection plus bounded `spec.md` anchoring under [spec_check_protocol.md](../deep-research/references/protocol/spec_check_protocol.md) |
414: | `/deep:start-review-loop` | N/A   | Autonomous code review loop with convergence detection                                                                           |
415: 
416: **Mode Suffixes** change how commands run:
417: 
418: | Suffix           | Behavior                                                                                              |
419: | ---------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
420: | `:auto`          | Execute without approval gates                                                                        |
421: | `:confirm`       | Pause at each step for approval                                                                       |
422: | `:with-phases`   | Phase decomposition mode on planning / completion flows, not a standalone command                     |
423: | `:with-research` | Dispatch deep research before verification (`/speckit:complete` only)                               |
424: 
425: **Command source files**: `.opencode/commands/speckit/`
426: 
427: #### Memory Commands (4)
428: 
429: | Command          | Tool Count | Purpose                                                                                                                                 |
430: | ---------------- | ---------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
431: | `/memory:save`   | 4          | Update packet continuity surfaces and supporting generated context artifacts with semantic indexing                                      |
432: | `/memory:search` | 13         | Search, retrieve and analyze knowledge. Auto-detects task intent from 7 types                                                           |
433: | `/memory:manage` | 20         | Database maintenance and lifecycle operations: stats, scan, cleanup, bulk-delete, checkpoints, and ingest |
434: | `/memory:learn`  | 6          | Constitutional memory manager: create, list, edit, remove always-surface rules                                                          |
435: 
436: Session recovery lives in `/speckit:resume`, which rebuilds packet context in this order: `handover.md`, then `_memory.continuity`, then canonical spec docs before deeper memory retrieval is needed.
437: 
438: Some commands own their tools (they are the primary home) while others borrow tools from `/memory:search` or `/memory:manage`. A borrowed tool works the same way -- it is just administered somewhere else.
439: 
440: **Command source files**: `.opencode/commands/memory/`
441:

445: 
446: ### 3.4 TEMPLATES
447: 
448: #### Manifest Architecture
449: 
450: Templates live in one manifest source and render through the Level contract resolver. `create.sh` asks the resolver which files belong to Level 1, Level 2, Level 3, Level 3+, or phase-parent packets, then the inline renderer expands only the sections allowed for that Level.
451: 
452: ```text
453: Level 1:  spec.md, plan.md, tasks.md, implementation-summary.md
454: Level 2:  Level 1 + checklist.md
455: Level 3:  Level 2 + decision-record.md
456: Level 3+: Level 3 + extended governance sections
457: Phase:    lean parent trio plus child phase folders
458: ```
459: 
460: Optional support documents such as `handover.md`, `debug-delegation.md`, `research.md`, and `resource-map.md` are rendered by the workflow that owns them. New spec folders should be created through the CLI:
461: 
462: ```bash
463: bash .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/scripts/spec/create.sh --level 2 --path /tmp/example-spec --name "example spec"
464: ```
465: 
466: Set `SPECKIT_POST_VALIDATE=1` when a strict workflow should run full validation immediately after scaffolding. The default path skips full post-create validation so normal scaffolds stay fast.
467: 
468: `create.sh` rejects `--path` values that traverse outside the repository (for example `--path "../etc/foo"`) with a clear error before any filesystem write happens.
469: 
470: A `mkdir`-based advisory lock protects `description.json` and `graph-metadata.json` writes during canonical save, so two parallel `/memory:save` calls for the same packet do not race.
471: 
472: #### Template Compliance
473: 
474: Templates use ANCHOR markers (`<!-- ANCHOR:section --> ... <!-- /ANCHOR:section -->`) to mark logical sections. Validation checks for required anchors, proper section ordering and template version alignment. The `template_compliance_contract.md` reference defines which anchors are required at each level.
475:

477:

479: 
480: ### 3.5 SCRIPTS AND VALIDATION
481: 
482: #### Spec Management Scripts
483: 
484: The `scripts/spec/` directory contains 12 scripts that manage the full lifecycle of spec folders:
485: 
486: | Script                        | Purpose                                                                                        |
487: | ----------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
488: | `create.sh`                   | Create spec folders with level-appropriate templates. Use `--phase` for parent + child folders |
489: | `validate.sh`                 | Run 20 validation rules. Use `--recursive` for phase folders, `--verbose` for details          |
490: | `upgrade-level.sh`            | Render additional Level contract sections for a higher documentation level                     |
491: | `recommend-level.sh`          | Analyze scope and risk to recommend the right documentation level                              |
492: | `calculate-completeness.sh`   | Calculate spec folder completeness as a percentage                                             |
493: | `check-completion.sh`         | Verify all completion criteria are met                                                         |
494: | `check-placeholders.sh`       | Find remaining `[PLACEHOLDER]` values after level upgrade                                      |
495: | `check-template-staleness.sh` | Detect templates that need regeneration                                                        |
496: | `progressive-validate.sh`     | Progressive validation for in-progress work                                                    |
497: | `quality-audit.sh`            | Run quality audit on spec folder content                                                       |
498: | `archive.sh`                  | Archive completed spec folders                                                                 |
499: | `test-validation.sh`          | Test the validation rules themselves                                                           |
500: 
501: #### Memory Scripts
502: 
503: The `scripts/memory/` directory contains 10 scripts for the indexed-continuity store:
504: 
505: | Script                        | Purpose                                                     |
506: | ----------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------- |
507: | `generate-context.ts`         | Source for the runtime memory-save entrypoint `scripts/dist/memory/generate-context.js` |
508: | `backfill-frontmatter.ts`     | Add missing frontmatter to existing generated context artifacts |
509: | `backfill-research-metadata.ts` | Backfill missing `description.json` and `graph-metadata.json` files under `research/*/iterations/` |
510: | `rank-memories.ts`            | Rank memories by relevance for a query                      |
511: | `reindex-embeddings.ts`       | Rebuild embedding vectors for stored spec-doc records               |
512: | `cleanup-orphaned-vectors.ts` | Remove vector entries with no matching spec-doc record               |
513: | `rebuild-auto-entities.ts`    | Regenerate auto-extracted entity catalog                    |
514: | `validate-memory-quality.ts`  | Run quality checks on stored spec-doc record content                 |
515: | `ast-parser.ts`               | Parse markdown AST for section extraction                   |
516: | `fix-memory-h1.mjs`           | Fix heading levels in older generated context artifacts     |
517: 
518: TypeScript sources compile to `scripts/dist/`. The runtime entry point for memory saves is `scripts/dist/memory/generate-context.js`.
519: 
520: #### Validation Helper Scripts
521: 
522: The `scripts/validation/` directory contains focused helpers that support `validate.sh` and one-off remediation work:
523: 
524: | Script                        | Purpose                                                                 |
525: | ----------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------- |
526: | `continuity-freshness.ts`     | Warn when `_memory.continuity.last_updated_at` lags `graph-metadata.json` |
527: | `evidence-marker-audit.ts`    | Bracket-depth audit and optional rewrap pass for malformed `EVIDENCE` markers |
528: | `evidence-marker-lint.ts`     | Strict wrapper used by validation to fail on malformed or unclosed markers |
529: 
530: #### Template Rendering
531: 
532: Template changes flow through the manifest source, Level contract resolver, and inline renderer. Use `create.sh` for generated packet fixtures and `validate.sh` for explicit validation.
533: 
534: ---
535: 
536: ## 4. STRUCTURE
537: 
538: ```
539: .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/
540: ├── SKILL.md                    # AI workflow instructions (when to use, gates, rules)
541: ├── README.md                   # This file (what it does, how to use it)
542: ├── ARCHITECTURE.md             # Boundary contract: scripts/ vs mcp_server/
543: ├── templates/                  # Manifest template source
544: │   └── manifest/               # Rendered by Level contract resolver + inline renderer
545: ├── scripts/                    # CLI tools (TypeScript source + Bash)
546: │   ├── spec/                   # Spec folder management (12 scripts)
547: │   ├── memory/                 # Memory system scripts (10 scripts)
548: │   ├── templates/              # Template composition (manifest renderer)
549: │   ├── core/                   # Core library (17 modules)
550: │   ├── extractors/             # Session data extractors (12 extractors)
551: │   ├── utils/                  # Utility modules (20 utilities)
552: │   └── dist/                   # Compiled JavaScript output
553: ├── mcp_server/                 # Spec Kit Memory MCP (TypeScript)
554: │   ├── context-server.ts       # MCP server entry point and tool registration
555: │   ├── handlers/               # Tool handlers, save pipeline, and response assembly
556: │   ├── lib/                    # Search pipeline, cognitive engine, graph, governance
557: │   ├── matrix_runners/         # F1-F14 x CLI adapter manifest and runner
558: │   ├── stress_test/            # Opt-in stress, load, matrix-cell, and degraded-state suites
559: │   ├── tests/                  # MCP test suite
560: │   ├── INSTALL_GUIDE.md        # Full installation walkthrough
561: │   └── README.md               # MCP server reference (tool API, pipeline, configuration)
562: ├── shared/                     # Shared workspace (@spec-kit/shared)
563: │   ├── algorithms/             # Fusion, reranking, and lab algorithms
564: │   ├── contracts/              # Typed trace/envelope contracts
565: │   ├── embeddings/             # Provider implementations
566: │   └── ...                     # Chunker, scoring, parsing, utilities
567: ├── references/                 # Reference documentation (27 files)
568: ├── assets/                     # Decision matrices, YAML configs
569: ├── constitutional/             # Always-surface rules (never decay)
570: ├── feature_catalog/            # Feature documentation (22 categories, 294 features)
571: └── manual_testing_playbook/    # Manual validation scenarios (22 categories, 316 scenario files)
572: ```
573: 
574: ### Key Files
575: 
576: | File                                                                         | Purpose                                                                                              |
577: | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
578: | [`SKILL.md`](./SKILL.md)                                                     | AI agent instructions: routing rules, gates, validation procedures, template application             |
579: | [`README.md`](./README.md)                                                   | This file -- what Spec Kit does, how to use it, where to find things                                 |
580: | [`ARCHITECTURE.md`](./ARCHITECTURE.md)                                       | API boundary contract between `scripts/` and `mcp_server/`                                           |
581: | [`mcp_server/README.md`](./mcp_server/README.md)                             | Full MCP architecture: 37-tool API reference, search pipeline, graph intelligence, and configuration |
582: | [`mcp_server/INSTALL_GUIDE.md`](./mcp_server/INSTALL_GUIDE.md)               | Step-by-step installation with embedding providers and environment                                   |
583: | [`scripts/spec/create.sh`](./scripts/spec/create.sh)                         | Create spec folders with level-appropriate template files                                            |
584: | [`scripts/spec/validate.sh`](./scripts/spec/validate.sh)                     | Run 20-rule validation on any spec folder                                                            |
585: | `scripts/dist/memory/generate-context.js`                                    | Primary workflow for updating packet continuity state from structured JSON                            |
586: | [`feature_catalog/feature_catalog.md`](./feature_catalog/feature_catalog.md) | Complete catalog of 294 implemented features across 22 categories                                    |
587: 
588: ### How the Pieces Connect
589: 
590: Think of Spec Kit as a filing system with a librarian attached.
591: 
592: The **spec folder workflow** is the filing system. Every time you modify files, it creates a numbered folder with the right paperwork (specification, plan, tasks). Templates make sure every folder follows the same structure. Validation checks that nothing is missing.
593: 
594: The **memory system** is the librarian. When a session ends, `generate-context.js` updates the packet's canonical continuity surfaces so the next session can recover from packet-local sources first. The MCP server indexes those packet docs into vector, FTS5, and BM25 surfaces, while graph and degree signals are computed at retrieval time. When a new session starts, `/speckit:resume` rebuilds context from `handover.md`, `_memory.continuity`, and the packet docs. If you need deeper retrieval after that, `session_bootstrap()` bundles resume context, health, and structural readiness into one follow-up recovery call before broader `memory_context` work begins.
595: 
596: The **commands** are the doors into the system. Each command opens access to the tools it needs. `/speckit:plan --intake-only` owns the standalone intake surface, `/speckit:plan` and `/speckit:complete` reuse the shared intake contract from [intake_contract.md](./references/workflows/intake_contract.md) when the Step 0 local `folder_state` requires delegation, and downstream callers should consume the returned `start_state` as the canonical intake enum. `/deep:start-research-loop` anchors research to `spec.md` through [spec_check_protocol.md](../deep-research/references/protocol/spec_check_protocol.md). `/memory:save` updates packet continuity. `/speckit:resume` recovers or continues a previous session.
597: 
598: The common packet lifecycle now uses `/speckit:plan --intake-only` for standalone trio creation or repair, `/deep:start-research-loop` can enrich that packet under the bounded `spec_check_protocol.md` rules, and `/speckit:plan` or `/speckit:complete` continue from the same folder without reopening intake unless the local `folder_state` still requires repair. When intake does run, `start_state` is the canonical downstream field.
599: 
600: ```text
601: Session starts
602:   └─► Gate 3 asks: "Which spec folder?"
603:        ├─► Option A: Use existing folder
604:        ├─► Option B: Create new folder (create.sh)
605:        └─► Option D: Skip documentation
606:             │
607:             ▼
608:   AI modifies files, tracks tasks in tasks.md
609:             │
610:             ▼
611:   Session ends
612:   └─► generate-context.js updates canonical continuity surfaces
613:        └─► MCP reindexes packet docs (vector + BM25 + graph)
614:             │
615:             ▼
616:   Next session starts
617:   └─► /speckit:resume reads handover.md -> _memory.continuity -> packet docs
618:        └─► session_bootstrap() or memory_context() deepen retrieval when needed
619:        └─► AI resumes with context + health + structural readiness
620: ```
621: 
622: ---
623: 
624: ## 5. CONFIGURATION
625: 
626: ### Embedding Providers
627: 
628: The indexed-continuity store converts text to numerical embeddings for vector search. Four providers are supported. The default cascade (when `EMBEDDINGS_PROVIDER=auto` or unset) is **local-first** (ADR-014): Ollama -> hf-local -> OpenAI -> Voyage.
629: 
630: | Tier | Provider          | Dimensions | Notes                                                            |
631: | ---- | ----------------- | ---------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------- |
632: | 1    | Ollama            | 768        | **Default.** Probes `/api/tags`; uses `nomic-embed-text-v1.5`. Recommended new-user setup. |
633: | 2    | HuggingFace Local | 768        | Pure-Node `@huggingface/transformers` HTTP model server (local-first fallback). Default model: `nomic-ai/nomic-embed-text-v1.5` (same family as the Ollama default, ADR-014). |
634: | 3    | OpenAI            | 1536       | Cloud opt-in. Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY`.                         |
635: | 4    | Voyage AI         | 1024       | Cloud opt-in. Requires `VOYAGE_API_KEY`. Gated by egress guard.  |
636: 
637: ### Environment Variables
638: 
639: | Variable             | Required    | Description                                          |
640: | -------------------- | ----------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |

(Output capped at 50 KB. Showing lines 1-640. Use offset=641 to continue.)
</content>

---
